title: TPU v1 · Flat Memory Hierarchy with a 24 MiB Unified Buffer
oneLine: A memory hierarchy need not be a stack of caches. TPU takes the opposite approach: a large software-managed on-chip SRAM plus off-chip DDR3, deliberately trading hardware-cache complexity for predictability and silicon area—a workload-specific extreme.

legend.name: Accumulators
legend.desc: The nearest, fastest, and smallest level: 4 MiB of 32-bit storage beside the MXU column outputs, dedicated to high-precision partial sums from 65,536 MACs.

legend.name: Unified Buffer
legend.desc: The main level is 24 MiB of on-chip SRAM. This software-managed scratchpad—not a hardware cache—holds activations and intermediates, with all movement scheduled statically by the compiler.

legend.name: Off-chip DDR3
legend.desc: The farthest, slowest, and largest level: 8 GiB at only ~30 GB/s, used mainly for model weights that stream through the Weight FIFO. It is v1's bandwidth bottleneck; v2+ adopted HBM.

legend.name: MXU (consumer)
legend.desc: The 256×256 systolic array drives every memory choice. Weights must remain resident and activations must arrive every cycle, so capacities and ports are derived from keeping this array fed.

legend.name: Hardware cache removed
legend.desc: TPU has no data cache, prefetcher, or cache-coherence machinery. Their area becomes SRAM and MACs, while software assumes full responsibility for data movement.

steps.cap: ① Fetch weights from 8 GiB of DDR3 while activations and intermediates reside in the 24 MiB Unified Buffer: large/slow storage for weights, medium/fast storage for activations.
② The Unified Buffer feeds every array row each cycle. As a scratchpad, transfer timing and volume are statically planned by the compiler—there are no cache misses.
③ Array partial sums enter the nearest level, 4 MiB of 32-bit accumulators; storage becomes smaller and faster closer to compute.
④ After activation and normalization, results return to the Unified Buffer as inputs to the next layer, avoiding DDR3 whenever possible.
⑤ No hardware cache participates. Saved area and power provide more SRAM and more deterministic latency.

before: Define the memory question: Does this chip need a traditional multilevel cache pyramid, where should data live, and who controls transfers? TPU replaces hardware caches with one very large software-managed SRAM.

Decisions to make: Total on-chip SRAM, allocation between activation storage and accumulators, off-chip memory type and bandwidth, cache versus scratchpad semantics, and read/write port widths at each level.

Required inputs: Working-set sizes from §3.1, including per-layer activations/intermediates and total weights; bandwidth and weight-residency needs from compute (§3.3); and SRAM density, area, and power from the process library.
Hard constraints: SRAM competes with MACs for area and power; off-chip bandwidth limits weight delivery; and data-center tail-latency SLAs favor deterministic behavior over cache variability.
during: Recognize predictable locality: Inference access patterns are regular and known at compile time, including each layer's activations and weights. Hardware that dynamically discovers locality is therefore unnecessary overhead.

Choose scratchpad over cache: Let XLA schedule explicit transfers into a software-managed scratchpad. Removing tags, comparators, coherence logic, and prefetchers yields deterministic latency and more usable storage.

Size and partition storage: Allocate 24 MiB to the Unified Buffer so most layer activations and intermediates stay on chip, plus 4 MiB of accumulators for 32-bit partial sums from 256 columns. Closer storage is smaller and faster.

Select off-chip memory: Weights are large but reused many times in a weight-stationary dataflow, amortizing bandwidth. TPU v1 therefore uses economical 8 GiB DDR3 at ~30 GB/s, which later became its main bottleneck and prompted HBM in v2+.
Balance bandwidth and capacity: The Unified Buffer must feed every array row each cycle, requiring wide read/write ports. Its size serves bandwidth and spill avoidance, not capacity alone.

Accept the software cost: Removing caches transfers complexity to the compiler. Every movement must be scheduled precisely; poor schedules starve the array. TPU exchanges software effort for deterministic hardware and usable area.

result.label: Hierarchy
result.value: Flat two-level design; no hardware cache or prefetch

result.label: Primary bottleneck
result.value: Off-chip bandwidth (addressed with HBM in v2+)

after: Validate the hierarchy: Use roofline and cycle-level models to see whether compute or Unified Buffer delivery limits utilization. Measure actual DDR3 traffic and weight reuse; revise SRAM partitioning or off-chip memory if targets are missed.

Propagate requirements: Specify Unified Buffer↔MXU bandwidth for interconnect (§3.5), establish compiler-controlled movement as the dataflow contract (§3.6), and give the software stack the hard ~30 GB/s off-chip limit.

Hand off to the compiler: A scratchpad cannot hide latency automatically. XLA must schedule weight preloads, activation streams, and result writes on a deterministic timeline; document this responsibility explicitly.
Add test and observability hooks: Provide counters for Unified Buffer reads/writes, DDR3 utilization, and data-starvation stalls, plus MBIST for large SRAM arrays.
Preserve an evolution path: Record v1's off-chip bandwidth bottleneck as the top next-generation improvement—the direct motivation for HBM in TPU v2/v3.
source: In-Datacenter Performance Analysis of a TPU, Jouppi et al., ISCA 2017. Facts disclosed in the paper are distinguished from illustrative examples and standard industry practice; the latter are not presented as disclosed TPU v1 implementation details.
